text.title: Cutover, Rollback, and Change Management
text.summary: Cutover checklist (T-24h through T+1w), rollback triggers and procedure, and change-management communications plan.

rule.statement: Validate three numbers before declaring any wave or cutover successful: issue count, comment count, and attachment count by byte.
rule.rationale: Issue count alone misses corrupted comments and missing attachments; the three-number check catches the most common silent data-loss patterns.

rule.statement: Cutover on a low-traffic window (Friday afternoon if org tolerates weekend quiet); never mid-week for large migrations.
rule.rationale: Weekend windows provide recovery time before full business-hours load resumes; mid-week cutovers interrupt peak productivity with no buffer.

list.item: T-24h: final data export from source, backup target environment, notify all users of timeline, prepare support team
T-4h: set source tool to read-only, run final migration sync, validate issue counts, test critical integrations
T-0: enable target tool for all users, send go-live announcement, support team on standby, monitor error rate
T+1h: verify user login success, confirm automations running, address immediate issues, update stakeholders
T+24h: review error logs, collect user feedback, document known issues, plan remediation
T+1w: measure adoption metrics, complete any missing migrations, conduct retrospective, plan source tool decommission date

antipattern.description: Agent marks checklist steps complete based on stdout text matches rather than process exit codes.
antipattern.reason: Text matches lie; a process can print "success" while returning exit code 1. Use exit codes for machine-verified steps.

rule.statement: Pre-write rollback communications before cutover begins so they can be sent within seconds of a decision to revert.
rule.rationale: Rollback decisions under stress are slowed by the need to draft communications; pre-writing removes this bottleneck and enables faster response.

list.item: Data loss detected → immediate rollback
Critical integration failure → immediate rollback
Team productivity drop ≥50% → rollback within 48 hours
User adoption under 30% at end of week 1 → rollback within 1 week
Pause migration — halt all ETL processes
Notify stakeholders immediately with pre-written message
Assess damage scope — what data was created only in target during cutover?
Restore source read access
Document failure root cause
Export any target-only data before reverting

rule.statement: Rollback only works if no critical data was created exclusively in the target during cutover; a single shipped feature with target-only context can pin you.
rule.rationale: Once teams start creating tickets in the new tool, those records don't exist in the source; rollback would lose that work, making true revert impossible.

list.item: T-4 weeks: migration announcement to all users via email + Slack + meeting
T-2 weeks: training schedule distributed via email + calendar invites
T-1 week: final preparation checklist via email + Slack
T-0: go-live announcement via email + Slack + meeting for high-impact teams
Daily for 2 weeks post-cutover: tips, FAQs, and support channel reminder via Slack

rule.statement: Rate-limit webhook-based notifications to one summary per migration phase; do not send a message per event.
rule.rationale: Retry storms in migration engines can trigger hundreds of webhook notifications, spamming channels and causing teams to mute the notifications needed for real alerts.

text.reference: Full project plan with scope, timeline, risk register, success criteria
